questions.questionText: Infant Mortality Rate (IMR) is defined as:
questions.optionA: Deaths in children under 5 per 1000 live births
questions.optionB: Number of deaths of infants under 1 year of age per 1000 live births in a given year
questions.optionC: Deaths in neonates per 1000 live births
questions.explanation: Core concept: IMR = deaths <1 year / 1000 live births → most sensitive index of health status and socioeconomic development. Why correct: IMR measures deaths from birth to 364 days per 1000 live births. Components: neonatal mortality rate (NMR: 0-28 days) + post-neonatal mortality rate (29 days to <1 year). India NFHS-5: IMR ~35/1000. Why A wrong: Under-5 mortality rate (U5MR) = deaths <5 years per 1000 live births. Why C wrong: NMR = neonatal deaths (0-28 days) per 1000 live births. Why D wrong: Stillbirth rate = fetal deaths (≥28 weeks gestation) per 1000 total births. Clinical relevance: NMR contributes ~70% of IMR in India. Main neonatal causes: prematurity, birth asphyxia, sepsis. Post-neonatal: diarrhea, pneumonia. Exam trap: IMR uses LIVE births as denominator (not total births). Stillbirths are NOT included in IMR. Memory aid: 'IMR = Infants (under 1) per Mille (1000) live births.' Reference: Park – MCH.
questions.conceptTag: IMR infant mortality under 1 live births

questions.questionText: Under the National Immunization Schedule (NIS) of India, BCG vaccine is given at:
questions.optionB: At birth (or as early as possible — single intradermal dose of 0.05 mL for neonates, left upper arm, deltoid insertion)
questions.explanation: Core concept: BCG is given at birth in India's NIS. Why correct: BCG (Bacillus Calmette-Guérin): live attenuated vaccine, given at birth or as soon as possible. Single dose, intradermal, left upper arm. Protects against severe forms of childhood TB (miliary TB, TB meningitis). Danish strain 1331 currently used. Why A wrong: 6 weeks = OPV-1, IPV-1, Pentavalent-1, Rotavirus-1, PCV-1. Why C wrong: 9 months = Measles-1 (MR vaccine), JE-1 (endemic areas). Why D wrong: 16 months = DPT-B1, OPV-B, MR-2. Clinical relevance: NIS birth doses: BCG + OPV-0 + Hep B birth dose. Tuberculin conversion: BCG scar appears in 3-6 weeks. No booster dose recommended in India. Exam trap: BCG is given INTRADERMAL (not subcutaneous or IM). It's the ONLY intradermal vaccine in NIS. Memory aid: 'BCG = Birth, intraCutaneous (dermal), Given on left deltoid.' Reference: Park – Immunization.
